literal: action, deed, rite; from √kṛ 'to do, to make'
invariant_definition: *Karma* is much broader than the English word 'action.' Its background lies in the grammar of agency: Pāṇinian *kāraka* theory analyzes a process through roles such as *kartṛ*, *karaṇa*, and *karman*, so *karma* already belongs to a structured account of who acts, by what means, and upon what. Philosophical schools then extend that grammar into causal theory. Mīmāṃsā treats *karma* as Veda-enjoined performance under *codanā* and *bhāvanā*, with *apūrva* explaining delayed ritual efficacy. Yoga and Vedānta foreground the sequence of action, residual trace, and fruition - *karma*, *saṃskāra*, and *vipāka* - while Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika often speaks of *adṛṣṭa* as the unseen carryover connecting deed and result. Thus *karma* can name the act, the ritual performance, the residual deposit left by action, or the whole economy by which embodied life is steered. Buddhists reconfigure it around intention and conditioned continuity without a permanent self; Jains construe it as karmic matter binding the *jīva*. Vedānta inherits that whole apparatus and classifies karmic residue as *sañcita*, *prārabdha*, and *āgāmi* while disputing whether *jñāna*, *bhakti*, or disciplined action is decisive for release.

per_school.definition: *Karma* belongs to the sphere of *avidyā*, since it presupposes *kartṛtva* and yields only anitya *kārya*; for that reason it cannot be a co-cause of *mokṣa* [[UpS I.11.15](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.11.15), [UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9), [PV p. 164](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahma-vicāre kim-ānantaryam atha-śabdāvagamyam), [NS 1.98-99](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.98-99)]. Its admissible role is preparatory rather than liberative: the Veda governs *adhikāra* and discipline, but liberation follows from the *mahāvākya*'s sentence-meaning, not from any *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* [[UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9), [PV p. 164](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahma-vicāre kim-ānantaryam atha-śabdāvagamyam), [NS 1.98-99](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/1.98-99)]. Where *kartṛtā* is ascribed with respect to *sukṛta* and *duṣkṛta* karma, it belongs to the *ātma-citi-pratibimba* in *buddhi*, not to the ātman in its own *svarūpa* [[SS I.327](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.327)].

Within Viśiṣṭādvaita, *kevala-karma* has only *alpa* and *asthira phala*, so the turn to *brahma-jijñāsā* begins from recognizing the insufficiency of ritual action taken by itself [[Śrī-Bh 3](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/3)]. Yet karmic fruition is fully real: the soul stands before Mukunda at the hour of *vipāka*, and the school's soteriological turn is therefore not the denial of karma but *prapatti* under the Lord's saving governance [[YStr 22-24](cite://yamuna/stotra-ratna/22-24)].

In Tattva-vāda, *karma* is real but never *svatantra*: even *dravya*, *karma*, *kāla*, *svabhāva*, and *jīva* stand only by Viṣṇu's *anugraha* [[Anuvy. 1.1.13-15](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15)]. Hence action is to be performed only when preceded by *bhagavad-bhakti*, *jñāna*, and *vairāgya*, and it reaches *siddhi* precisely as *sva-karma* transformed into Viṣṇu-arcana [[KN 257a](cite://madhva/karma-nirnaya/p. 257a), [KN 257c](cite://madhva/karma-nirnaya/p. 257c)]. Unoffered karma remains *abhadra*, whereas faithful adherence to the Lord's teaching releases one from binding karmas [[KN 257b](cite://madhva/karma-nirnaya/p. 257b), [KN 257e](cite://madhva/karma-nirnaya/p. 257e)].

In Acintya-Bhedābheda, karma is a real bondage-knot (*karma-granthi*) in the dependent life of the *jīva*, but mantra-centered worship joined to *bhakti* is precisely the mode in which that knot is cut [[KD 8.96](cite://kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/8.96)]. Keśava Kāśmīrī states the sequence explicitly: *japa*, *huta*, and *yajana* are effective as *bhakti-namra* practice, and their consummation is Murāri's *nitya-śuddha pada* once all karma-bondage is severed [[KD 8.96](cite://kesava-kasmiri/krama-dipika/8.96)].

Yoga places karma within the chain *kleśa-karma-vipāka-āśaya*: action, its ripening, and its latent deposit are constitutive of saṃsāric entanglement, whereas Īśvara alone is untouched by them [[YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)]. Accordingly karmic process is not an external ledger but an internal causal economy linking affliction, action, fruition, and residual seed until yogic discipline brings that economy to cessation [[YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)].

For Mādhyamaka, karma is not an ultimately self-grounding moral substrate but a dependently arisen complex inseparable from *kleśa*, *vikalpa*, and *prapañca* [[MMK 18.5](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5)]. Its exhaustion belongs to the cessation of proliferative construction in *śūnyatā*, so karmic bondage is undone not by reifying a doer but by dismantling the conceptual fabric from which karma and affliction arise [[MMK 18.5](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.5)].

In Yogācāra, karma persists as *vāsanā* within the *vijñāna-saṃtāna* rather than as the deed of a permanent self, and its fruit must arise where that perfuming is lodged [[Vvs 6-8](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/Karma, Vāsanā, and Scriptural Objection), [Triṃśikā 19](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/19)]. The school accordingly explains shared worlds and rebirth through karmic *vipāka* and the continuing generation of further *vipāka* from prior perfumings together with the dualizing *grāhadvaya-vāsanā* [[Vvs 3](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Preta Replies), [Vvs 4](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Hell), [Vvs 6-8](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/Karma, Vāsanā, and Scriptural Objection), [Triṃśikā 19](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/19)].

translator_note: Avoid the lazy glosses 'work,' 'fate,' and 'what goes around comes around.' 'Work' strips away the causal and ritual apparatus; 'fate' turns *karma* into passive destiny; the popular proverb makes it sound like a vague moral boomerang. *Karma* names an action-structure and its residual efficacy inside a theory of agency, causation, and rebirth.
